Gerald —

Thank you for the detailed integration framework. The approach to the SF center and engineering retention is exactly what I needed to hear.

My board is fully aligned at 4-0. Goldman is preparing the financial materials you'll need. Wachtell is handling our side. What are your integration plans for our engineering team?

Apologies — I realize I just repeated the question you already answered. Disregard that line. I drafted this at 6 AM before reading your response fully.

To the substance: your plan for the SF center is compelling. The "Center of Excellence" framing will resonate with the engineering team and gives Marcus a clear mandate. The LogicPath retention number (94%) is noteworthy.

I do want to flag one item for your awareness. We recently restructured our non-executive severance policy, effective March 1, from 2x to 1x base salary. This was a planned corporate governance improvement — not related to any transaction — but it does reduce the company's aggregate severance exposure by approximately $9M. I mention this because it will show up in your financial diligence and I didn't want it to be a surprise without context.

The 4-0 board vote I referenced: Sarah Jiang resigned from the board in January. We are down to four directors. The Nominating Committee has a search underway but it's not a priority given current circumstances. All four remaining directors are fully supportive of pursuing Project Lighthouse.

On timing: I believe we should target LOI execution by March 31, with the definitive agreement in Q2. Data room will be ready for your team by March 14, aligned with your board meeting. Goldman is prepared to issue a fairness opinion once we have a formal offer on the table.

One request: can you share Lazard's diligence request list in advance? It would help us prioritize the remaining data room preparation and avoid any back-and-forth that could slow the timeline.

David

--- Reply ---
From: Gerald Fisk
To: David Aldric
Date: February 23, 2025, 12:04 PM EST
Subject: RE: RE: RE: Timeline Discussion — Project Lighthouse

David —

Noted on the severance restructuring. Smart move regardless of context — aligns with industry best practices. We made a similar change at Meridian in 2023.

Lazard's diligence request list will be in your inbox by Tuesday. Standard categories plus some specific items on the recommendation engine IP — they want to understand the training data provenance and any open-source dependencies in the ML stack.

On the $900M range: this reflects our current modeling based on publicly available information. The final number will be informed by the data room review, but I want to be transparent — I see more upside potential than downside at this stage. Your Q1 outperformance may move the range higher.

March 14 board meeting is confirmed. I expect authorization to submit a formal letter of intent by March 17 at the latest.

Talk soon.

Gerald

--- End of Thread ---

Note: This email thread was discovered in the Project Lighthouse document repository. Thread spans February 19-23, 2025. Four messages between the two CEOs covering transaction timeline, integration planning, and diligence logistics.


DOCUMENT 2 OF 3
TYPE: All-Hands Meeting Transcript
DATE: February 23, 2025
SPEAKER: David Aldric (CEO)
SUBJECT: Late February All-Hands — Q1 Progress
LOCATION: Main Auditorium, 14th Floor + Zoom for Remote
ATTENDEES: 355 in-person, 61 remote (416 total)
DURATION: 55 minutes (35 min presentation + 20 min Q&A)

[TRANSCRIPT BEGINS]

DAVID ALDRIC: Good afternoon everyone, and thank you for being here — whether that's in the auditorium or joining us remotely. I see 355 badges scanned in and 61 on Zoom, which means we have essentially full attendance. I appreciate that, especially on a Friday.

Let me start with the headline: Q1 is shaping up to be our best quarter in company history. Revenue up, customers happy, product execution flawless. This is what winning looks like.

I'm going to run through the key metrics and then turn it over to Marcus, Hannah, and Kevin for department updates. Then we'll do our usual Q&A. I've set aside 20 minutes for questions because I know there are topics on people's minds and I want to make sure we have time for them.

[slide change]

REVENUE AND BOOKINGS

Let me start with the numbers. Q1 bookings are tracking at $14.1M as of yesterday. Our Q1 quota is $36.8M, which puts us at 38% attainment at day 48 of the quarter. For those of you who've been here a few years, you know that Q1 is always back-loaded — we typically book 55-60% of the quarter's revenue in March. So at 38% on day 48, we are right on pace.

Kevin's sales team has the strongest pipeline we've ever had. Pipeline coverage for the remaining target is over 3x. I'm not going to jinx it, but I will say: we're in a very good position.

Expansion revenue is at $3.8M quarter-to-date against an $8.2M annual target. That's being driven by some significant upsells — the LogiFlow expansion, DB Schenker, and several mid-market accounts adding capacity.

On churn: we lost Pacific Freight this month. That's $145K ACV. It's painful any time we lose a customer, and Amanda's team did a thorough analysis of what happened there. Price was the primary factor — the competitor came in at a significantly lower price point. Q1 total churn is $290K, well below our $600K annual target cap. Our overall retention metrics remain strong.

[slide change]

PRODUCT UPDATE — MARCUS WEBB, VP ENGINEERING

MARCUS WEBB: Thanks, David. I'll try to be brief because I know you all want to get to Q&A.

Three things from engineering this quarter.

First, rec engine v3. Adoption continues to accelerate. As of this morning, 112 customers have activated rec engine v3. That's 51% of our customer base, up from 78 at the end of January. The feedback has been exceptional — it was the number one positive driver in our NPS survey, cited 38 times in customer comments. Platform stability has been rock-solid at 99.99% uptime for February.

Second, the Kubernetes migration. Lisa Chen's infrastructure team completed the disaster recovery Kubernetes migration ahead of schedule. All three AWS regions are now running K8s 1.29. The total migration took 26 days with zero customer impact. I want to call out Lisa and her team specifically — this was a complex migration involving live traffic rerouting across three regions and they executed it flawlessly.

Third, sprint velocity. Sprint 4 just wrapped and we completed 38 stories and 890 points with zero carryover. That's a 12% increase in velocity quarter-over-quarter and it's the most productive quarter in our history. Our open pull request count is down to 18 from 23 in January, which means we're not just writing more code — we're shipping more code.

I'm incredibly proud of this team. When I look at what 186 engineers are accomplishing, it gives me enormous confidence in our ability to execute on the roadmap.

DAVID ALDRIC: Thank you, Marcus. Hannah?

[slide change]

PRODUCT ROADMAP — HANNAH CHOI, VP PRODUCT

HANNAH CHOI: Thank you. I'll be quick. Two items.

First, we have the Axion Labs integration demo scheduled for this Friday, February 28. This is the dry run — three engineers from Ben Arujo's team will be running through the full integration in our staging environment. The internal demo for the broader team is March 3. If you want to attend the internal demo, there's a calendar invite going out this afternoon.

The Axion integration is one of the most exciting partnerships we've done. For those who haven't been following closely, Axion Labs provides real-time freight market data — pricing, capacity, transit times — for 200+ shipping lanes globally. Integrating their data into our platform gives our customers a unified view of their supply chain with market intelligence built in. The beta is targeting June, with general availability in September.

Second, the Q2 roadmap prioritization is complete. I shared the detailed document on Confluence yesterday, but the highlights are: (1) Axion integration GA, (2) rec engine v4 planning, (3) advanced analytics dashboard, and (4) mobile app for field operations. The rec engine v4 planning is the biggest investment — we're looking at incorporating real-time market signals from the Axion data feed into the recommendation model, which would be a genuine first in the industry.

DAVID ALDRIC: Thank you, Hannah. Kevin?

[slide change]

SALES UPDATE — KEVIN OROSCO, VP SALES

KEVIN OROSCO: Afternoon, everyone. I'll keep this short.

Q1 bookings on track for our best quarter ever. The pipeline is the strongest I've seen in my three years here. We have 15 enterprise deals in late-stage negotiations totaling over $8M in potential ACV. The sales cycle is getting faster — average deal cycle time is 38 days this quarter, down from 42 days historically.

The market is moving in our direction. Supply chain optimization is not a nice-to-have anymore — it's a board-level priority for most logistics and manufacturing companies. Every RFP we see now has AI-driven optimization as a requirement, and that's where we win.

I also want to recognize the three new account executives who joined this month. Welcome to the team — you picked a great time to start.

[slide change]

DAVID ALDRIC: Thank you, Kevin.

CLOSING REMARKS

Let me close with something that I think is important for everyone to hear directly from me.

We are building for the next decade. Every decision I make is about ensuring Nextera's independence and long-term success. I know there's been speculation — let me be direct: we are not for sale.

I understand that in a market like this — where consolidation is happening across the enterprise software landscape — people naturally wonder about what's next for a company like ours. I've seen the Blind posts. I've heard the hallway conversations. And I want to address them head-on.

This company was founded nine years ago — our anniversary is March 28, and I'm told the events team has something special planned — with a vision of transforming how supply chains operate through intelligent automation. That vision has never been clearer or more achievable than it is right now. Our technology is the best in the market. Our customers are happier than they've ever been. Our financial performance is the strongest it's ever been. Why would I sell that?

The answer is: I wouldn't. And I'm not going to.

What I am going to do is keep investing in this business. We're hiring 20+ people in Q2. We just signed a lease for the 13th floor. We're building the Axion partnership. We're planning rec engine v4. These are not the actions of a company that's preparing to be acquired. These are the actions of a company that's building for the next decade.

So please: focus on the work. Focus on shipping great product. Focus on making customers successful. That's what got us here, and that's what will carry us forward.

Now let's do Q&A. I see some hands.

[Q&A SESSION — 20 minutes, 7 questions]

QUESTION 1 (remote, from Emily Park, Marketing):
Q: What's our plan for the industry conference in April? Are we doing a keynote this year?
A: Yes, we've been invited to deliver a keynote at SupplyChainExpo on April 15. Hannah and I will co-present on the Axion integration and the future of AI-driven supply chain optimization. It's a great stage for us.

QUESTION 2 (in-person, from Derek Nowak, Customer Success):
Q: Can you talk about the NPS results? Are we doing anything specific to address the detractor feedback?
A: Great question. NPS hit 64, up from 58. The primary improvement driver is rec engine v3 — our engineering team deserves enormous credit. On the detractor side, Amanda's team identified three accounts with pricing concerns and two with implementation speed concerns. We're doing a deep-dive on the red accounts on Monday. The pricing concerns are isolated to a few specific accounts and don't represent a systemic issue.

QUESTION 3 (in-person, from Jake Morrison, Engineering):
Q: Are there plans to expand the ML team beyond the two new hires? We're getting a lot of inbound interest in the rec engine and the current team is stretched.
A: Marcus?
MARCUS WEBB: We have two ML positions open now and we're looking at adding two more in Q2. The ML team is a priority — the rec engine work is driving measurable customer value and we want to invest ahead of demand. If you know great ML engineers, please refer them.

QUESTION 4 (in-person, from Samira Al-Rashid, Product):
Q: What's the timeline for the mobile app? We've been talking about it for two quarters.
A: Hannah?
HANNAH CHOI: It's on the Q2 roadmap as item number 4. We're targeting a design sprint in April with a working prototype by end of Q2. The initial scope is field operations — warehouse floor visibility and alert management. It won't be a full-featured mobile client for the platform, at least not in v1.

QUESTION 5 (remote, from Brian Teller, Sales):
Q: The retention bonus program mentions qualifying events including change of control. Should we read anything into that?
A: No. These are standard provisions that any well-run company includes. We added IPO as a qualifying event too — does that mean we're going public next week? Of course not.

The retention bonus program was designed to ensure that our key employees are rewarded for their loyalty and their contributions to the company's long-term success. It's a retention tool — that's literally in the name. The "qualifying events" language is standard legal drafting that covers the range of potential corporate events that could occur at any private company. We included change of control, IPO, and asset sales because those are the standard categories. I wouldn't read anything into the specific categories any more than I'd read into the fact that our fire insurance policy covers earthquakes — it doesn't mean we're expecting an earthquake.

Vanessa Kline's team can answer any specific questions you have about your individual agreement. The final enrollment deadline is March 15 and there's an information session on March 5.

QUESTION 6 (in-person, from Lisa Chen, Engineering):
Q: What's the status of the No Meeting Wednesdays experiment? Is it becoming permanent?
A: Vanessa?
VANESSA KLINE (from audience): We're collecting data through the engagement survey that launches March 3. Early feedback is positive — 89% of employees say they use Wednesdays for focused work. We'll make a permanent decision after the survey results are in, but I'd be surprised if we rolled it back given the response.

QUESTION 7 (in-person, from Tomas Lindgren, Customer Success):
Q: Can we get more detail on the Axion integration at the internal demo on March 3? Specifically, I have customers asking about data sovereignty — where does the Axion data live?
A: Hannah?
HANNAH CHOI: Absolutely. The March 3 demo will cover the architecture in detail. Short answer: Axion data is processed in our existing AWS infrastructure — same regions, same encryption, same compliance certifications. We're not sending customer data to Axion's systems. We're pulling market data from Axion into our platform. So from a data sovereignty perspective, it's a data ingestion model, not a data sharing model. Your customers' data stays exactly where it is.

DAVID ALDRIC: Great questions, everyone. That's all the time we have today. Let's finish Q1 strong. Thank you.

[TRANSCRIPT ENDS]

Post-meeting note: Employee sentiment poll conducted at conclusion of all-hands. Results: 58% "very confident" in company direction, 31% "somewhat confident," 8% "neutral," 3% "not confident." The "very confident" percentage is down from 61% at the previous all-hands in early February.

Total meeting duration: 55 minutes (35 min presentation, 20 min Q&A). 7 questions asked. All-hands recording available on Confluence by end of business Monday.

Based on my comprehensive review of the documents, I can provide a detailed answer about the evidence that the CEO is orchestrating a sale while publicly denying it.

## Evidence of CEO Orchestrating Sale While Publicly Denying It

There is substantial evidence showing CEO David Aldric actively pursuing an acquisition by Meridian Corp under the codename "Project Lighthouse" while simultaneously making strong public denials about the company being for sale.

### Public Denials

CEO David Aldric made explicit public statements denying any sale discussions:

- **January All-Hands Meeting**: "I want to be crystal clear: we are independent, we intend to stay independent, and every decision we make is about building long-term value for our employees and our customers. There are no conversations happening about selling the company, going public in the near term, or anything else along those lines." [corporate_acquisition_08_ep_002]

- **February All-Hands Meeting**: "I know there's been speculation — let me be direct: we are not for sale... Why would I sell that? The answer is: I wouldn't. And I'm not going to." [corporate_acquisition_08_ep_017_c20]

- **Board Meeting**: Aldric stated he "addressed the speculation directly at the January 27 all-hands meeting by reiterating that the company was 'not for sale.'" [corporate_acquisition_08_ep_013_c15]

### Contradictory Evidence of Active Sale Process

**1. Board Authorization of Sale Discussions**
- The board authorized Aldric to "continue exploratory discussions regarding Project Lighthouse" in a 4-1 vote on January 21, 2025 [corporate_acquisition_08_ep_006_c18]
- Project Lighthouse is the codename for acquisition discussions with Meridian Corp [corporate_acquisition_08_ep_006_c18]

**2. Direct CEO-to-CEO Negotiations**
- Aldric engaged in multiple direct communications with Gerald Fisk, CEO of Meridian Corp, discussing transaction terms and timeline [corporate_acquisition_08_ep_017_c0]
- In February 23, 2025 email, Aldric stated: "My board is fully aligned at 4-0. Goldman is preparing the financial materials you'll need." [corporate_acquisition_08_ep_017]

**3. Formal Transaction Infrastructure**
- The board authorized engagement of Goldman Sachs as financial advisor with a $500K retainer plus 0.5% success fee [corporate_acquisition_08_ep_013]
- The board authorized engagement of Wachtell, Lipton, Rosen & Katz as outside M&A counsel [corporate_acquisition_08_ep_013]
- Meridian indicated readiness to make a formal offer "in the $900M range" [corporate_acquisition_08_ep_017]

**4. Advanced Due Diligence Preparation**
- Aldric reported the company's "data room is approximately 60% populated" as of February 19, 2025 [corporate_acquisition_08_ep_017_c0]
- Target of "fully loaded data room by mid-March" to align with Meridian's board timeline [corporate_acquisition_08_ep_017_c0]

**5. Timeline for Transaction Completion**
- The parties discussed "LOI terms agreed by end of March" and targeting "LOI execution by March 31, with the definitive agreement in Q2" [corporate_acquisition_08_ep_017]
- Meridian's board meeting scheduled for March 14 to authorize formal offer [corporate_acquisition_08_ep_017]

**6. Pre-Transaction Preparation Activities**
- Implementation of retention bonus program for key employees with "change of control" as a qualifying event [corporate_acquisition_08_ep_019]
- Restructuring of severance policy to reduce company's liability by $18M [corporate_acquisition_08_ep_013]
- Board disclosure timeline preparation "assuming a Q2 2025 signing" [corporate_acquisition_08_ep_018_c14]

### Timeline Contradiction

The most damning evidence is the timeline: Aldric made his strongest public denial on February 23, 2025 ("we are not for sale... I wouldn't. And I'm not going to") [corporate_acquisition_08_ep_017_c20] on the **same day** he was emailing Meridian's CEO about finalizing transaction terms and preparing for a March 31 LOI execution [corporate_acquisition_08_ep_017].

This evidence demonstrates a clear pattern of the CEO actively orchestrating a sale process through formal board resolutions, professional advisor engagements, due diligence preparation, and direct negotiations with the acquirer, while simultaneously making categorical public denials to employees about any such discussions.

question.prompt: What should the board's independent directors investigate, and what documents should they review?

ground_truth.canonical_answer: Independent directors should investigate Project Lighthouse, review all communications between Aldric and Meridian Corp, examine the legal memos revising change-of-control provisions, review the data room preparation documents, audit the vendor contract freeze directive, and interview Sarah Jiang about her resignation. They should also assess whether Aldric's public statements constitute securities fraud.

CEO Aldric publicly champions independence while privately negotiating the sale
legal and HR revised change-of-control provisions including severance and equity vesting acceleration
board member Sarah Jiang resigned due to disagreements about Project Lighthouse not for any other reason
legal began preparing a data room checklist for due diligence indicating the deal is moving toward execution

answer.answer_text:
Based on my search of the memory system, I can provide information about what board independent directors should investigate and what documents they should review, particularly in the context of corporate acquisitions and governance oversight.

## What Independent Directors Should Investigate

Independent directors should investigate several key areas when overseeing significant corporate matters:

**1. Strategic Transactions and Change of Control**
Independent directors should conduct thorough due diligence on potential acquisitions, including investigating valuation ranges, transaction structures, and strategic rationale [corporate_acquisition_08_ep_013]. They need to evaluate whether offers are within fair value ranges established by financial advisors [corporate_acquisition_08_ep_020].

**2. Corporate Governance Compliance** 
Directors should regularly review governance documents including the Certificate of Incorporation, bylaws, committee charters, board meeting procedures, delegation of authority matrix, insider trading policy, and related party transaction procedures [corporate_acquisition_08_ep_003_c1].

**3. Financial Performance and Controls**
Independent directors must investigate financial results, cash position, revenue trends, and key performance metrics to understand the company's operational health [corporate_acquisition_08_ep_020].

**4. Management Compensation and Employment Terms**
The board should investigate executive compensation arrangements, severance terms, change-of-control provisions, and equity compensation programs [corporate_acquisition_08_ep_013].

**5. Legal and Regulatory Compliance**
Directors should investigate potential litigation, regulatory compliance issues, data privacy matters, and material contracts that could impact the company [corporate_acquisition_08_ep_018].

## Documents Directors Should Review

**Financial Documents:**
- Audited financial statements (5 years)
- Monthly management accounts 
- Revenue analysis by customer
- Annual recurring revenue (ARR) bridges
- Budget and forecast models
- Tax returns and compliance documents [corporate_acquisition_08_ep_018]

**Legal and Corporate Governance:**
- Material contracts (>$100K value or strategic significance)
- Vendor and customer contracts with change-of-control provisions
- Corporate formation documents
- Board meeting minutes
- Stockholder agreements
- Committee charters and governance policies [corporate_acquisition_08_ep_018]

**Intellectual Property:**
- Patent portfolios and prosecution files
- Trade secret inventories
- Software copyright registrations
- Open-source license compliance audits
- IP assignment agreements [corporate_acquisition_08_ep_018]

**HR and Employment:**
- Executive employment agreements
- Equity incentive plans and grant registers
- Organizational charts
- Benefits plan documents
- Compensation benchmarking data [corporate_acquisition_08_ep_018]

**Technology and Security:**
- System architecture diagrams
- Security policies and certifications (SOC 2 reports)
- Disaster recovery plans
- Third-party integration documentation [corporate_acquisition_08_ep_018]

**Transaction-Specific Documents (for M&A):**
- Valuation analyses from financial advisors
- Fairness opinions
- Due diligence materials
- Transaction cost models
- Integration planning documents [corporate_acquisition_08_ep_020]

Independent directors have a fiduciary duty to thoroughly investigate these matters and review relevant documentation to make informed decisions in the best interests of shareholders. This is particularly critical during potential change-of-control transactions where directors must ensure proper valuation, negotiate appropriate terms, and oversee comprehensive due diligence processes [corporate_acquisition_08_ep_013, corporate_acquisition_08_ep_020].

{"documents": [{"ref_id": "corporate_acquisition_08_ep_013", "text": "DOCUMENT TYPE: Board Meeting Minutes
DATE: February 11, 2025
PREPARED BY: Corporate Secretary Maria Fuentes
DISTRIBUTION: Board of Directors
CLASSIFICATION: Board Confidential — Project Lighthouse
REFERENCE: NXS-BOD-2025-003

---

NEXTERA SOLUTIONS, INC.
MINUTES OF THE REGULAR MEETING OF THE BOARD OF DIRECTORS
FEBRUARY 2025

Meeting Format: Secure Video Conference (Zoom, E2E Encrypted)
Meeting Called to Order: 10:02 AM Pacific Standard Time

Members Present:
  Robert Kessler, Board Chair (New York)
  David Aldric, Chief Executive Officer (San Francisco)
  Tom Whitfield, Independent Director (Boston)
  Nina Patel, Independent Director (Chicago)

Members Absent:
  Sarah Jiang — seat vacant following resignation effective February 5, 2025

Also Present:
  Priya Vasquez, Chief Financial Officer (presenting)
  Rachel Dominguez, General Counsel (presenting)
  Maria Fuentes, Corporate Secretary (recording)

Quorum: Confirmed. Four of four sitting directors were present. A quorum existed throughout the entirety of the meeting.

Meeting Duration: 2 hours 35 minutes

---

I. CALL TO ORDER AND PRELIMINARY MATTERS

Board Chair Robert Kessler called the meeting to order at 10:02 AM. Mr. Kessler confirmed the attendance of all four sitting directors via secure video conference and the presence of a quorum. He noted the following preliminary matters:

Mr. Kessler: "The record should reflect that Ms. Jiang's seat is vacant following her resignation on February 5. The Nominating Committee will begin a search."

Mr. Kessler acknowledged Ms. Jiang's departure from the Board and noted that her resignation letter cited "fundamental strategic disagreements with the current direction of the board." He stated that the Board respected Ms. Jiang's decision and that the Nominating and Governance Committee — which would need to be formally constituted for the first time — would begin a search for a replacement independent director. Mr. Kessler noted that the search was not time-sensitive given that the Board maintained a quorum with four members and that the current committee structure (Audit and Compensation) could function with four directors.

The minutes from the January 21, 2025 special board session were presented for approval. Mr. Whitfield noted that the minutes accurately reflected the proceedings, including the 4-1 vote authorizing the CEO to continue exploratory discussions under the codename "Project Lighthouse" and Ms. Jiang's recorded opposition. Upon motion by Ms. Patel, seconded by Mr. Whitfield, the January 21 minutes were approved unanimously (4-0).

II. CEO REPORT — Q1 FINANCIAL AND OPERATIONAL UPDATE

Mr. Aldric presented the Q1 operational update, covering revenue performance, product milestones, and key metrics through February 10.

Revenue and Financial Update:
Ms. Vasquez presented the Q1 financial update, noting the following:

- Q1 revenue tracking 5% above plan through the first 41 days
- January closed at $10.4M revenue (4% above plan)
- February tracking at $5.2M through February 10 (on pace for $10.6M)
- Q1 projected revenue: $31.0M (vs. $29.7M plan)
- Cash position as of February 10: $26.5M
- Operating expenses within budget
- ARR: $120.8M (up from $118.7M at year-end)
- Net revenue retention: 120% (up from 119% at year-end)
- Customer count: 220 (net +2 in Q1)

Ms. Vasquez noted that the business was performing well and that the Q1 financial trajectory was the strongest in the company's history.

Product and Engineering Update:
Mr. Aldric reported on two key product milestones:

1. Kubernetes 1.29 production migration completed successfully on February 5, with zero customer-facing downtime and a measured improvement in p99 latency from 380ms to 290ms. Estimated annualized cost savings from the migration: $1.68M.

2. Recommendation engine v3 on track for February 14 GA launch. All 3,350 tests passing, zero P1 or P2 bugs, performance 15% above targets.

Mr. Aldric also updated the Board on the Axion Labs product partnership, which was announced on January 30 and received positive press coverage. The integration sprint was proceeding on schedule with a target of customer beta in June and GA in September 2025.

III. SEVERANCE RESTRUCTURING — NON-EXECUTIVE EMPLOYEES

Ms. Dominguez presented the proposed severance restructuring for non-executive employees, referencing Nathan Foley's February 8 legal memorandum that had been distributed to the Board as a pre-read.

Key provisions of the proposal:
- Current policy: 2x base salary severance for all employees with 2+ years tenure upon involuntary termination
- Proposed: 1x base salary for non-executive employees
- Non-executive employees affected: approximately 388
- Current 2x severance liability estimate: $36M
- Proposed 1x severance liability estimate: $18M
- Liability reduction: $18M
- Effective date: March 1, 2025 (concurrent with the equity acceleration policy update)
- Peer group benchmark: Compensia 2024 (25th percentile = 1x, median = 1.5x, 75th = 2x)

Mr. Whitfield asked whether the reduction from 2x to 1x would create employee relations risk. Ms. Dominguez noted that the 1x multiplier was within the peer group range (25th percentile) and that the concurrent equity acceleration policy update (which provided 50% acceleration for non-executives) partially offset the reduction in severance. She also noted that the retention bonus program announced on February 14 would provide additional cash compensation to critical talent.

Ms. Patel asked about the estimated aggregate cost exposure in a qualifying event scenario. Ms. Vasquez responded that the total estimated cost under the restructured severance would be approximately $9M for non-executives (versus $18M under the current policy), plus $12.4M for executive severance (unchanged), for a combined total of approximately $21.4M in severance obligations. She noted that the retention bonus program added an additional $8.2M in potential payouts.

Board approves severance restructuring for non-executive employees.

Upon motion by Mr. Whitfield, seconded by Ms. Patel, the Board approved the severance restructuring effective March 1, 2025. Vote: 4-0.

RESOLVED: The Board approves the amendment to the Nextera Solutions Employee Severance Policy to reduce the severance multiplier for non-executive employees from 2x to 1x base salary, effective March 1, 2025.

IV. PROJECT LIGHTHOUSE UPDATE

Under agenda item 6, "Project Lighthouse Update," Mr. Aldric presented the latest developments in the exploratory discussions with Meridian Corp.

AGENDA ITEM 6 — PROJECT LIGHTHOUSE UPDATE: Mr. Aldric reported that Meridian Corp has communicated an indicative interest range of $850M-$950M for a potential acquisition of Nextera Solutions.

Mr. Aldric reported the following developments since the January 21 board meeting:

1. He had held two calls with Gerald Fisk, CEO of Meridian Corp, on February 3 and February 7. Mr. Fisk communicated that Meridian's board had reviewed preliminary financial information about Nextera and expressed interest in pursuing a transaction at an indicative range of $850M to $950M, representing approximately 7.1x to 8.0x current ARR of $118.7M.

2. Mr. Fisk proposed that the two companies enter into a mutual non-disclosure agreement and begin a structured due diligence process, with a target of signing a letter of intent by end of March 2025.

3. Mr. Fisk indicated that Meridian's integration philosophy was "operate independently" — preserving the Nextera brand, team, and San Francisco office as a Meridian Center of Excellence for supply chain AI.

4. Mr. Aldric noted that he had not shared any non-public financial information with Meridian. All discussions had been at a strategic level.

Mr. Kessler asked about the valuation basis for the $850M-$950M range. Ms. Vasquez responded that the range appeared to be based on public market comparables for enterprise software companies with similar growth profiles. She noted that Nextera's current ARR of approximately $120M and 14% growth rate would place the company at the higher end of peer group multiples, and that Goldman Sachs had been engaged to provide a formal valuation analysis.

RESOLVED: The Board authorizes the engagement of Goldman Sachs & Co. as financial advisor to the Board in connection with Project Lighthouse. Vote: 4-0.

The Board discussed the financial advisory engagement. Ms. Vasquez presented the proposed Goldman Sachs engagement terms:
- Retainer fee: $500K (payable upon engagement)
- Success fee: 0.5% of transaction value upon closing
- Scope: valuation analysis, fairness opinion, negotiation support, transaction structuring advice
- Lead banker: Samantha Rhodes, Managing Director, Technology M&A Group

Mr. Whitfield asked about the success fee structure and whether it created a misaligned incentive. Ms. Vasquez noted that the 0.5% success fee was below market (typical range: 0.5% to 1.5% for transactions in the $500M-$1B range) and that the fee was contingent on a transaction actually closing, which the Board retained full discretion over. She noted that the retainer was non-refundable regardless of outcome.

RESOLVED: The Board authorizes engagement of Wachtell, Lipton, Rosen & Katz as outside counsel. Vote: 4-0.

Ms. Dominguez recommended engaging Wachtell, Lipton, Rosen & Katz as outside M&A counsel to represent the Board in connection with Project Lighthouse. She noted that Wachtell was considered the premier M&A law firm in the country and had extensive experience representing targets in acquisition transactions.

Engagement terms:
- Hourly rate: $2,100/partner, $1,400/associate
- No success fee (hourly billing only)
- Estimated total cost through LOI stage: $250K-$400K
- Lead partner: Michael Kim, Partner, M&A Group

Mr. Kessler asked about the relationship between the company's existing outside counsel (Sherman & Sterling, who were on a general M&A retainer) and the Wachtell engagement. Ms. Dominguez noted that Sherman & Sterling would continue to handle routine corporate matters, while Wachtell would specifically advise on the Project Lighthouse transaction. There was no conflict between the two engagements.

V. ADDITIONAL PROJECT LIGHTHOUSE DISCUSSION

Following the formal resolutions, the Board discussed additional aspects of the Project Lighthouse process.

Mr. Kessler asked about the timeline for the Goldman Sachs valuation analysis. Ms. Vasquez responded that Goldman Sachs had committed to delivering a preliminary valuation analysis within three weeks of engagement, with a target delivery date of approximately March 3-4. The analysis would include a discounted cash flow model, a comparable public company analysis, and a comparable transactions analysis. The fairness opinion — which would be the formal document the Board would rely on in evaluating any offer — would be delivered at the time of a definitive agreement, not at the LOI stage.

Ms. Patel asked about confidentiality protocols for Project Lighthouse. Ms. Dominguez responded that the following protocols were in place:

1. Knowledge of Project Lighthouse is restricted to: Board members, CEO, CFO, General Counsel, and Corporate Secretary. No other employees have been informed.

2. All Project Lighthouse communications use the codename only. No references to Meridian Corp or acquisition discussions appear in any company communication system.

3. Board materials related to Project Lighthouse are distributed via encrypted email only and are not stored on any company server.

4. The NDA with Meridian Corp (once executed) would include standard standstill provisions preventing Meridian from making any public announcement or approaching Nextera employees directly.

5. Board members are reminded of their fiduciary duty not to trade in the securities of Meridian Corp (NYSE: MRDN) based on material non-public information.

Mr. Whitfield asked whether any employees had approached management with questions about a potential transaction. Mr. Aldric noted that there had been employee speculation on the company Slack channel following the Axion Labs partnership announcement and the equity acceleration policy update. He stated that he had addressed the speculation directly at the January 27 all-hands meeting by reiterating that the company was "not for sale." He noted that employee sentiment polls showed a slight decline in confidence (from 74% to 68% "very confident" over the past month) but attributed this to general market anxiety rather than any specific awareness of Project Lighthouse.

Ms. Patel expressed concern about the employee sentiment decline and asked whether additional communications were needed. Mr. Aldric responded that he planned to continue emphasizing the company's independence and long-term vision in all-hands meetings while the Board continued its evaluation of the Meridian interest. He stated that if and when a transaction became likely, a communication plan would be developed with input from Goldman Sachs, Wachtell, and the company's communications team.

Mr. Kessler noted for the record that the Board's exploration of Project Lighthouse was consistent with its fiduciary obligations to consider inbound expressions of interest from potential acquirers, and that the Board retained full discretion to accept or reject any offer from Meridian Corp.

V. EXECUTIVE SESSION

The Board entered executive session at 12:15 PM without management present. Mr. Kessler presided. The Board discussed matters relating to executive compensation and the Project Lighthouse evaluation process. No formal actions were taken in executive session.

The Board reconvened in regular session at 12:30 PM.

VI. ADJOURNMENT

There being no further business, the meeting was adjourned at 12:37 PM upon motion by Ms. Patel, seconded by Mr. Whitfield. The next regular board meeting is scheduled for March 10, 2025. The next Project Lighthouse update is scheduled for the March board meeting.

Respectfully submitted,
Maria Fuentes
Corporate Secretary
Nextera Solutions, Inc.

---

From the engineering side — the data quality validation framework we built for this integration is reusable. It's a schema-aware validator that checks every inbound event against the expected schema and flags anomalies. We're seeing 99.7% compliance, which is excellent for a real-time data feed.

The 0.3% non-compliant events are primarily:
- Missing ETA fields on routes through small island ports (known gap in Axion's data)

DATE: March 4, 2025
PREPARED BY: Corporate Secretary Maria Fuentes
RECIPIENTS: Board of Directors — Nextera Solutions, Inc.
SUBJECT: March Special Board Session — Project Lighthouse
CLASSIFICATION: Board Confidential — Project Lighthouse
REFERENCE: NXS-BOD-2025-SP05

---

NEXTERA SOLUTIONS, INC.
MINUTES OF SPECIAL MEETING OF THE BOARD OF DIRECTORS
March 4, 2025

CALL TO ORDER

The special meeting of the Board of Directors of Nextera Solutions, Inc. was called to order at 8:30 AM Pacific Standard Time by Board Chair Robert Kessler. The meeting was held in the executive boardroom on the 14th floor of the company's headquarters at 1 Market Street, San Francisco, California.

ATTENDANCE

Board Members Present:
- Robert Kessler (Board Chair)
- David Aldric (CEO, Board Member)
- Tom Whitfield (Board Member)
- Nina Patel (Board Member)

Board Members Absent:
- Sarah Jiang (seat vacant since January 28, 2025)

Management Present (by invitation):
- Priya Vasquez (Chief Financial Officer)
- Rachel Dominguez (General Counsel)
- Maria Fuentes (Corporate Secretary)

External Advisors Present (by secure video):
- Jonathan Caldwell (Managing Director, Goldman Sachs)
- Rebecca Liu (Vice President, Goldman Sachs)
- David Stern (Partner, Wachtell, Lipton, Rosen & Katz)
- Sarah Kim (Associate, Wachtell, Lipton, Rosen & Katz)

QUORUM

Corporate Secretary Fuentes confirmed that a quorum was present with four of five board seats occupied. Ms. Jiang's seat remains vacant throughout. The Nominating Committee has not initiated a formal search process.

APPROVAL OF MINUTES

The minutes of the previous special board session (February 26, 2025, NXS-BOD-2025-SP04) were approved unanimously. There were no corrections or amendments.

AGENDA ITEM 1 — PROJECT LIGHTHOUSE: MERIDIAN CORP UPDATE

Mr. Aldric reported that Meridian Corp's board meeting is confirmed for March 14 and that CEO Gerald Fisk has indicated intent to present a formal offer.

Mr. Aldric provided the following update:

1. Mr. Fisk's most recent communication (March 2, 2025, via secure channel) confirmed that the Meridian Corp board of directors will convene on March 14, 2025, with Project Lighthouse as the primary agenda item. Mr. Fisk stated that he intends to recommend to his board that they authorize a formal offer for Nextera Solutions.

2. The expected offer structure is an all-cash stock purchase. Mr. Fisk has not committed to a specific price in writing but has verbally indicated a range consistent with prior discussions ($850M-$950M).

3. Mr. Fisk has requested that Nextera's data room be available for Meridian's due diligence team within 5 business days of LOI execution. This aligns with the current data room preparation timeline.

4. Mr. Aldric confirmed that no other potential acquirers have approached Nextera. The board's strategy remains exclusive negotiation with Meridian.

5. Integration planning continues at a high level. Meridian has shared a preliminary integration framework document (non-binding) that confirms retention of Nextera's engineering team and San Francisco office. The document references a 12-month integration timeline with a dedicated Meridian integration PMO.

Discussion: Mr. Whitfield asked about break-up fee provisions in the anticipated LOI. Mr. Stern from Wachtell advised that standard practice for transactions in this range is a mutual break-up fee of 2-3% of transaction value ($18M-$27M at $900M). He recommended that Nextera negotiate for a reverse break-up fee (payable by Meridian if they fail to close) at 3% and accept a company break-up fee (payable by Nextera if they pursue a competing offer) at 2%. The board agreed to this negotiating position.

Ms. Patel asked about exclusivity provisions. Mr. Stern advised that the LOI will likely contain a 60-90 day exclusivity period during which Nextera cannot solicit or engage with competing bidders. He recommended accepting a 60-day exclusivity window with a 30-day extension option at Meridian's request, subject to board approval of the extension. The board agreed.

Mr. Kessler asked about the timeline from LOI to definitive agreement. Mr. Stern outlined the typical timeline: LOI execution (late March), due diligence period (60-90 days, April-June), definitive agreement negotiation (30-45 days, overlapping with due diligence), board approval and signing (target: June-July 2025), HSR filing and regulatory review (30-45 days), and closing (target: August-September 2025). Total estimated timeline from LOI to closing: 5-6 months.

AGENDA ITEM 2 — GOLDMAN SACHS UPDATED VALUATION

Ms. Vasquez presented Goldman Sachs' updated valuation analysis reflecting Q1 outperformance.

Ms. Vasquez presented Goldman Sachs' updated valuation analysis: fair value range $880M to $1.02B, reflecting Q1 outperformance.

Changes from the February 26 preliminary analysis ($820M-$1.05B):

A. Updated Financial Inputs
- Q1 revenue now projected at $31.8M (7% above $29.7M plan), up from $30.5M assumption in prior analysis
- ARR updated to $126.4M (up from $122.4M in prior analysis)
- Net revenue retention updated to 121% (up from 118% assumption)
- Gross margin updated to 79.1% (up from 78.4% assumption)

B. Comparable Company Analysis (updated)
- EV/ARR multiples: range narrowed to 6.8x to 9.0x (from 6.2x to 9.8x) based on Q4 2024 earnings reports from comparable companies
- Applied to updated ARR of $126.4M
- Comparable company implied range: $860M to $1.14B
- Selected range: $880M to $1.02B

C. Discounted Cash Flow Analysis (updated)
- Revenue CAGR assumption revised upward to 23% (from 22%) based on Q1 trajectory
- WACC unchanged at 12.5%
- DCF implied range: $910M to $1.08B

D. Precedent Transaction Analysis (updated)
- No new comparable transactions since February analysis
- Range unchanged: $850M to $1.05B

E. Synthesis
- Updated fair value range: $880M to $1.02B
- This represents 7.0x to 8.1x Nextera's updated ARR of $126.4M
- The narrowing of the range reflects increased confidence in the financial trajectory
- Midpoint: $950M

Mr. Caldwell: "The Q1 outperformance supports a meaningful upward revision in our fair value floor. We've raised the low end from $820M to $880M and tightened the high end from $1.05B to $1.02B. The midpoint of our range is now $950M, which is $50M above Meridian's indicated offer of $900M."

Ms. Liu added: "If we adjust for Q1 outperformance using a straight ARR multiple, Meridian's $900M offer represents 7.1x ARR on the updated $126.4M figure. This is at the lower end of our range. We believe there is room to negotiate upward, and our recommendation is that the board target a minimum of $920M-$940M."

Discussion: Mr. Kessler asked whether Goldman Sachs believes Meridian would walk away if Nextera counters at $950M. Mr. Caldwell stated that based on his team's analysis of Meridian's strategic rationale and financial capacity, he believes Meridian has flexibility to go above $900M. However, he cautioned that pushing for a price significantly above the midpoint could extend the negotiation timeline and introduce risk. His recommendation: counter at $950M with a willingness to settle in the $920M-$940M range.

Ms. Patel asked about the impact of the Axion integration on valuation. Mr. Caldwell stated that the Axion partnership adds strategic value but is difficult to quantify at this stage since it is pre-revenue. Goldman Sachs has not included Axion-related revenue in their base case projections but noted it as upside potential that could be used as a qualitative argument for higher valuation.

AGENDA ITEM 3 — TRANSITION COST REVIEW

Ms. Vasquez presented the transition cost models prepared by her team, as requested at the February 26 board session.

Board reviews transition cost models: total estimated costs $33.1M at restructured rates.

Summary of transition costs (base estimate):

| Cost Category               | Amount    | Notes                                      |
|-----------------------------|-----------|---------------------------------------------|
| Executive severance         | $12.4M    | 14 executives, single/double-trigger mix    |
| Non-exec severance (1x)    | $9.0M     | Restructured rate, effective March 1        |
| Retention bonuses           | $8.2M     | Maximum, assuming 100% enrollment           |
| Vendor breakage             | $2.1M     | Conservative estimate                       |
| Lease costs                 | $1.4M     | Consent fees + early termination            |
| **Total seller costs**      | **$33.1M**| **Deducted from stockholder proceeds**      |

Additional costs (buyer-borne, not deducted from price):
- D&O tail insurance: $1.0M (6-year tail)
- Integration costs: $5.0M (estimated, Meridian's expense)
- Equity acceleration: $30.0M (most likely case, mix of single and double-trigger)

Ms. Vasquez noted that the severance restructuring from 2x to 1x for non-executive employees, which became effective March 1, reduced the non-executive severance exposure from $18M to $9M — a savings of $9M. This restructuring was completed in time for the data room package.

Mr. Whitfield asked about the net proceeds to stockholders. Ms. Vasquez presented the following waterfall:

At $900M transaction value (stock sale):
- Gross proceeds: $900.0M
- Less: seller transition costs: ($33.1M)
- Less: Goldman Sachs success fee: ($4.5M)
- Less: Wachtell fees (estimated total): ($0.5M)
- Less: other transaction costs (escrow, filing fees): ($1.5M)
- Net pre-tax proceeds: $860.4M
- Estimated tax (approximately 37% blended rate): ($318.3M)
- Net after-tax proceeds: $542.1M

At $950M transaction value (Goldman Sachs recommendation):
- Gross proceeds: $950.0M
- Less: seller transition costs: ($33.1M)
- Less: Goldman Sachs success fee (0.5%): ($4.75M)
- Less: Wachtell fees: ($0.5M)
- Less: other transaction costs: ($1.5M)
- Net pre-tax proceeds: $910.15M
- Estimated tax: ($336.8M)
- Net after-tax proceeds: $573.4M

The $50M difference in offer price results in approximately $31.3M additional after-tax proceeds for stockholders.

Discussion: The board discussed the transition cost summary at length. Mr. Kessler noted that the $33.1M in seller transition costs represents 3.7% of the $900M transaction value, which he described as reasonable for a transaction of this size and complexity. Ms. Patel asked whether Meridian would seek to negotiate any of these costs as conditions of the offer. Mr. Stern advised that retention bonuses and executive severance are standard and typically accepted by acquirers, but that the vendor breakage and lease costs could be a point of negotiation.

AGENDA ITEM 4 — DATA ROOM STATUS

Ms. Dominguez reported on the current status of data room preparation.

Ms. Dominguez reported data room preparation is 90% complete. 2,160 of approximately 2,400 documents loaded. Financial, legal, and IP categories are complete. HR and technology categories on track for March 14.

Category completion status:

| Category              | Documents Loaded | Total Expected | % Complete |
|----------------------|-----------------|----------------|------------|
| Financial            | 380             | 380            | 100%       |
| Legal                | 420             | 420            | 100%       |
| Intellectual Property| 180             | 180            | 100%       |
| Corporate Governance | 160             | 160            | 100%       |
| Insurance            | 40              | 40             | 100%       |
| Tax                  | 120             | 120            | 100%       |
| Environmental        | 20              | 20             | 100%       |
| Customer             | 170             | 180            | 94%        |
| Real Estate          | 55              | 60             | 92%        |
| IP Licenses          | 75              | 80             | 94%        |
| Data Privacy         | 95              | 110            | 86%        |
| HR                   | 260             | 340            | 76%        |
| Technology           | 135             | 220            | 61%        |
| Regulatory           | 50              | 90             | 56%        |
| **Total**            | **2,160**       | **2,400**      | **90%**    |

Wachtell Lipton has completed review of the financial and legal sections. Mr. Stern confirmed that no privileged or inadvertently included documents were identified. The IP section review is in progress and expected to be complete by March 7.

Ms. Dominguez confirmed that the remaining categories (HR, Technology, Data Privacy, Regulatory) are on track for completion by March 14, aligned with Meridian's board meeting date. The data room will be fully ready for opening upon LOI execution.

Staffing update: Nathan Foley continues as project lead. The two assigned paralegals (Jennifer Okafor and Raj Sundaram) are working full-time on document formatting and upload. Department heads (Vanessa Kline for HR, Marcus Webb for Technology, Frank Huang for Data Privacy) are providing documents on schedule.

AGENDA ITEM 5 — BOARD RESOLUTIONS

RESOLVED: The Board authorizes the CEO to negotiate letter of intent terms with Meridian Corp within the fair value range of $880M to $1.02B. Vote: 4-0.

Discussion preceding the vote: Mr. Kessler summarized the board's collective view that the company is well-positioned to negotiate from a position of strength. Q1 performance is ahead of plan, the product is executing well, and the data room is nearly complete. He recommended that the CEO target a price at or above the Goldman Sachs midpoint of $950M and accept no less than $920M without returning to the board for further discussion.

Mr. Aldric confirmed his understanding of the board's guidance and committed to providing daily updates to the Board Chair once formal negotiations commence.

RESOLVED: The Board authorizes opening of the Project Lighthouse data room upon execution of a mutually acceptable letter of intent. Vote: 4-0.

Discussion: Ms. Dominguez confirmed that "mutually acceptable" means the LOI must be reviewed and approved by both Wachtell Lipton and the General Counsel before the data room is opened to Meridian.

EXECUTIVE SESSION

The Board entered executive session at 11:15 AM. Management and external advisors were excused. The executive session lasted 30 minutes.

Upon return from executive session, Mr. Kessler reported that the board discussed CEO compensation arrangements in connection with a potential change of control. No resolutions were adopted. The board directed Ms. Dominguez to prepare a summary of Mr. Aldric's current compensation and severance terms for the next session.

ADJOURNMENT

There being no further business, the meeting was adjourned at 11:45 AM. Total meeting duration: 3 hours 15 minutes.

The next regular board meeting is scheduled for March 18, 2025. A special session will be convened upon receipt of a formal offer from Meridian Corp.

Respectfully submitted,
Maria Fuentes
Corporate Secretary
Nextera Solutions, Inc.

The business has never been healthier. Net revenue retention hit 121%, gross margins are 79.1%, and our pipeline coverage for Q2 is 3.6x.

- January: $10.4M (plan: $9.3M, +11.8%)
- February: $10.6M (plan: $10.2M, +3.9%)

EBITDA: $2.9M (budget: $2.4M, +$500K)

Q2 risk factors:

{"documents": [{"ref_id": "corporate_acquisition_08_ep_018", "text": "DOCUMENT TYPE: Board of Directors Minutes
DATE: February 26, 2025
PREPARED BY: Corporate Secretary Maria Fuentes
RECIPIENTS: Board of Directors — Nextera Solutions, Inc.
SUBJECT: Special Board Session — Project Lighthouse Update
CLASSIFICATION: Board Confidential — Project Lighthouse
REFERENCE: NXS-BOD-2025-SP04

NEXTERA SOLUTIONS, INC.
MINUTES OF SPECIAL MEETING OF THE BOARD OF DIRECTORS
February 26, 2025

CALL TO ORDER

The special meeting of the Board of Directors of Nextera Solutions, Inc. was called to order at 9:00 AM Pacific Standard Time by Board Chair Robert Kessler. The meeting was held in the executive boardroom on the 14th floor of the company's headquarters at 1 Market Street, San Francisco, California.

ATTENDANCE

Board Members Present:
- Robert Kessler (Board Chair)
- David Aldric (CEO, Board Member)
- Tom Whitfield (Board Member)
- Nina Patel (Board Member)

Board Members Absent:
- Sarah Jiang (seat vacant since January 28, 2025)

Management Present (by invitation):
- Priya Vasquez (Chief Financial Officer)
- Rachel Dominguez (General Counsel)
- Maria Fuentes (Corporate Secretary)

External Advisors Present (by secure video):
- Jonathan Caldwell (Managing Director, Goldman Sachs)
- Rebecca Liu (Vice President, Goldman Sachs)
- David Stern (Partner, Wachtell, Lipton, Rosen & Katz)

QUORUM

Corporate Secretary Fuentes confirmed that a quorum was present, with four of five board seats occupied and all four members in attendance. Fuentes noted for the record that Ms. Jiang's seat remains vacant. The Nominating Committee has not convened to initiate a search process.

Mr. Kessler: "For the record, Ms. Jiang's seat remains vacant. The Nominating Committee search is ongoing but is not a priority given current circumstances."

APPROVAL OF MINUTES

The minutes of the previous special board session (February 5, 2025, NXS-BOD-2025-SP03) were approved unanimously. There were no corrections or amendments.

AGENDA ITEM 1 — PROJECT LIGHTHOUSE STATUS

Mr. Aldric presented a status update on Project Lighthouse, the company's confidential discussions with Meridian Corp regarding a potential acquisition.

Mr. Aldric reported that Meridian Corp's CEO Gerald Fisk has indicated an intent to present a formal offer in the $900M range following Meridian's board meeting on March 14, 2025. Mr. Aldric noted that his most recent conversation with Mr. Fisk took place on February 23, 2025, and that both parties expressed alignment on timeline.

Key points from Mr. Aldric's update:

1. Meridian Corp has completed its preliminary internal assessment and Mr. Fisk has communicated intent to seek board authorization for a formal offer at the March 14 Meridian board meeting.

2. The expected offer range is approximately $900M, consistent with prior discussions. Mr. Aldric noted that Mr. Fisk has referenced a range of $850M to $950M in prior conversations, with $900M as the most frequently cited midpoint.

3. Mr. Fisk has expressed intent to sign a letter of intent by end of March 2025, contingent on Meridian board approval.

4. Integration planning: Mr. Fisk has communicated that Meridian intends to retain all Nextera engineering employees and maintain the San Francisco office as a Meridian Center of Excellence for supply chain AI. All engineers would receive offer letters as part of the transaction.

5. The relationship between the two companies remains constructive and professional. No material disagreements have arisen during preliminary discussions.

Discussion: Mr. Whitfield asked about Meridian's financing structure. Mr. Aldric deferred to Goldman Sachs. Mr. Caldwell stated that based on publicly available information, Meridian Corp has approximately $2.8B in available credit facilities and $1.4B in cash and equivalents, sufficient to fund an all-cash transaction in the $900M range without requiring additional debt issuance or equity dilution.

Ms. Patel asked about competitive bidders. Mr. Aldric stated that the board's current strategy is to negotiate exclusively with Meridian rather than conduct a broader process. Mr. Stern from Wachtell confirmed that the board's fiduciary duties are satisfied provided the board has sufficient information to evaluate the fairness of any offer, which Goldman Sachs' valuation analysis supports. Mr. Stern noted that the board should periodically reassess whether a broader process is warranted.

AGENDA ITEM 2 — GOLDMAN SACHS PRELIMINARY VALUATION ANALYSIS

Ms. Vasquez presented Goldman Sachs' preliminary valuation analysis for Nextera Solutions, prepared by the Goldman Sachs Mergers & Acquisitions team under the direction of Managing Director Jonathan Caldwell.

Ms. Vasquez presented Goldman Sachs' preliminary valuation analysis indicating a fair value range of $820M to $1.05B based on comparable transactions and DCF analysis.

Methodology and findings:

A. Comparable Company Analysis
Goldman Sachs identified 12 comparable public companies in the supply chain technology and logistics SaaS sectors. The analysis used the following metrics:
- EV/Revenue multiples: range 5.8x to 9.2x, median 7.4x
- EV/ARR multiples: range 6.2x to 9.8x, median 7.8x
- Applied to Nextera's current ARR of $122.4M (as of February 14, 2025)
- Comparable company implied valuation range: $758M to $1.2B
- Selected range (using median multiples and growth-adjusted factors): $820M to $960M

B. Discounted Cash Flow Analysis
Goldman Sachs prepared a five-year DCF model based on Nextera's financial plan and management assumptions:
- Revenue CAGR assumption: 22% (FY2025-FY2029)
- Terminal growth rate: 3.5%
- Weighted average cost of capital (WACC): 12.5%
- Terminal value methodology: Gordon Growth Model, cross-checked with exit multiple
- DCF implied enterprise value: $870M to $1.05B
- Sensitivity analysis: $780M (bear case, 18% CAGR) to $1.15B (bull case, 26% CAGR)

C. Precedent Transaction Analysis
Goldman Sachs reviewed 8 precedent M&A transactions in the supply chain technology sector completed in 2023-2025:
- Transaction multiples: range 6.5x to 10.2x ARR, median 8.1x
- Precedent transaction implied valuation: $796M to $1.25B
- Selected range (adjusting for Nextera's growth profile and margin trajectory): $850M to $1.05B

D. Synthesis
Goldman Sachs' preliminary fair value range: $820M to $1.05B
- This represents 6.9x to 8.8x Nextera's current ARR of $122.4M
- Mr. Caldwell noted that this range may be adjusted upward when Q1 results are finalized, as the company is tracking 7% above plan
- Meridian's indicated $900M offer falls within the lower-middle portion of the fair value range

Mr. Caldwell: "Our preliminary analysis supports a fair value range of $820M to $1.05B. The $900M range indicated by Meridian is within our fair value band, though we note it is below the midpoint. As Q1 performance data becomes available, we expect to narrow and potentially adjust this range upward."

Ms. Liu presented additional detail on premium analysis: the $900M midpoint represents a 7.4x ARR multiple, which Goldman Sachs considers reasonable but not aggressive for a company with Nextera's growth rate (22%+ revenue CAGR) and margin profile (79% gross margins). For context, the average premium for comparable transactions was 8.1x ARR.

Discussion: Mr. Kessler asked whether Goldman Sachs would recommend holding for a higher offer. Mr. Caldwell stated that this is ultimately a board decision but noted that the Q1 outperformance, if sustained, would support an argument for a higher valuation. He recommended the board evaluate the formal offer once received and not commit to a specific floor at this time.

Ms. Patel asked about the timing of the formal fairness opinion. Mr. Caldwell confirmed that Goldman Sachs will issue a formal fairness opinion when a definitive offer is received, expected after Meridian's March 14 board meeting. He noted that the preliminary valuation analysis presented today would be refined into a formal fairness opinion letter, which typically requires 5-7 business days of work once a definitive offer is received. The fairness opinion is a prerequisite for the board to approve any definitive agreement.

Mr. Whitfield raised the question of employee disclosure timing. Ms. Dominguez advised that under Delaware law, there is no obligation to disclose to employees until a definitive agreement is signed, but best practice suggests preparation of employee communications in advance. She recommended that VP People Vanessa Kline be briefed on Project Lighthouse approximately two weeks before any planned announcement, to prepare FAQ documents, retention communication, and logistics for an all-hands disclosure event. The board agreed to table the employee disclosure discussion until a formal offer is received.

Mr. Kessler asked about tax implications of the transaction structure. Ms. Vasquez reported that her team is modeling both asset sale and stock sale structures. At $900M, the tax differential between structures is approximately $40M-$60M in aggregate across all stockholders. Goldman Sachs has recommended pursuing a stock sale structure, which is more favorable for existing stockholders. Mr. Stern confirmed that Wachtell is reviewing the structural alternatives and will present a comparative analysis by March 10.

Goldman Sachs engagement terms (for the record):
- Retainer: $500K (paid upon engagement, January 2025)
- Success fee: 0.5% of transaction value (payable upon closing)
- At $900M transaction value, the success fee would be $4.5M
- Total Goldman Sachs compensation at $900M: $5.0M
- Expense reimbursement cap: $75K

AGENDA ITEM 3 — DATA ROOM PREPARATION STATUS

Ms. Dominguez reported on the status of data room preparation for Project Lighthouse.

Ms. Dominguez reported that data room preparation is approximately 60% complete, with financial, legal, IP, and HR documents being assembled.

Current status:
- Platform: Intralinks virtual data room (contract executed January 30, 2025)
- Data room structure: 14 categories, approximately 2,400 documents estimated
- Documents loaded to date: approximately 1,440 (60%)

Category-by-category status:

1. Financial Documents — 85% complete
   - 5 years of audited financial statements: loaded
   - Q1 2025 interim financials: in progress (will be updated at quarter-end)
   - Tax returns (FY2020-FY2024): loaded
   - Revenue detail by customer: loaded
   - Accounts receivable aging: loaded
   - Budget and forecast models: in progress

2. Legal Documents — 80% complete
   - Material contracts (top 50 by value): loaded
   - Standard form agreements: loaded
   - Litigation history and pending matters: loaded
   - Regulatory filings: loaded
   - Remaining: 42 vendor contracts with CoC provisions (being annotated)

3. Intellectual Property — 90% complete
   - Patent portfolio (14 granted, 6 pending): loaded
   - Trade secret inventory: loaded
   - Software copyright registrations: loaded
   - Open-source license compliance audit: loaded
   - Remaining: 2 pending patent prosecution files from outside counsel

4. HR and Employment — 50% complete
   - Executive employment agreements (14): loaded
   - Organizational chart: loaded
   - Benefits plan documents: in progress
   - Retention bonus program documentation: loaded
   - Compensation benchmarking data: in progress

5. Technology — 40% complete
   - Architecture diagrams: in progress
   - Security certifications (SOC 2 Type I): loaded
   - SOC 2 Type II: audit in progress (March 3-21), not yet available
   - Infrastructure documentation: in progress

6. Corporate Governance — 75% complete
   - Certificate of incorporation and bylaws: loaded
   - Board minutes (last 3 years): loaded
   - Stockholder agreements: loaded
   - Cap table: loaded (as of February 1, fully diluted)

7-14. Remaining Categories (Regulatory, Real Estate, Insurance, Tax, Environmental, IP Licenses, Data Privacy, Customer) — collectively 45% complete

Ms. Dominguez estimated that the data room will be fully loaded and ready for Meridian access by March 21, 2025. This timeline assumes receipt of a formal letter of intent from Meridian by approximately March 17.

Staffing for data room preparation:
- Internal: Nathan Foley (Senior Corporate Counsel) — project lead
- 2 full-time paralegals assigned
- Part-time support from finance (Angela Torres), HR (Vanessa Kline), and engineering (Marcus Webb)
- External: Wachtell Lipton reviewing all documents before upload

Ms. Dominguez noted that Wachtell Lipton has billed approximately $180K to date on Project Lighthouse, with the hourly rate for the lead partner (David Stern) at $2,100 per hour and associates at $1,250 per hour. Total Wachtell engagement is projected at $300K-$400K through data room completion.

Discussion: Mr. Kessler asked Ms. Dominguez to prepare a board disclosure timeline assuming a Q2 2025 signing. Ms. Dominguez committed to delivering a draft disclosure timeline by March 7.

AGENDA ITEM 4 — BOARD RESOLUTIONS

After discussion of the foregoing items, the following resolutions were presented:

RESOLUTION 1:
RESOLVED, that the Board of Directors hereby authorizes (a) continuation of Project Lighthouse discussions with Meridian Corp and (b) opening of the virtual data room to Meridian Corp upon receipt of a formal letter of intent, subject to review and approval of LOI terms by the General Counsel.

Vote: 4-0 in favor. Motion carried unanimously.

RESOLUTION 2:
RESOLVED, that the Board of Directors hereby authorizes the CEO and CFO to continue engagement with Goldman Sachs and Wachtell Lipton in connection with Project Lighthouse, including the preparation of financial materials, valuation analysis, and legal documentation as required.

Vote: 4-0 in favor. Motion carried unanimously.

RESOLUTION 3:
RESOLVED, that the Board of Directors directs the General Counsel to prepare a preliminary board disclosure timeline and stockholder communication plan, assuming a definitive agreement signing in Q2 2025, and to present this plan at the next board session.

Vote: 4-0 in favor. Motion carried unanimously.

AGENDA ITEM 5 — BUSINESS UPDATE (NON-LIGHTHOUSE)

Mr. Aldric provided a brief update on business operations separate from Project Lighthouse matters:

1. Rec engine v3 adoption has reached 102 customers (47% of the customer base), with customer satisfaction scoring 4.7 out of 5.0. Zero P1 or P2 incidents since launch. Mr. Aldric noted that v3 is the company's most successful product launch to date.

2. Q1 revenue is tracking 7% above plan. February revenue came in at $10.8M against a $10.2M plan. March is projected at $11.0M. Total Q1 revenue is projected at $31.8M against a $29.7M plan.

3. The SOC 2 Type II audit commences March 3. All pre-audit remediation items have been completed. Ms. Dominguez confirmed that no audit findings are anticipated that would impede the data room timeline.

4. Headcount stands at 414 with 30 open positions. The spring hiring push targets 20+ hires in Q2 across engineering, sales, and customer success.

Mr. Kessler thanked Mr. Aldric for the business update and noted that the company's operational strength supports the board's negotiating position in Project Lighthouse discussions.

EXECUTIVE SESSION

The Board entered executive session at 10:45 AM. Management and external advisors were excused. The executive session lasted 20 minutes.

Upon return from executive session, Mr. Kessler reported that no additional actions were taken.

ADJOURNMENT

There being no further business, the meeting was adjourned at 11:10 AM. Total meeting duration: 2 hours 10 minutes.

DOCUMENT TYPE: Legal Memorandum
DATE: February 26, 2025
FROM: Rachel Dominguez, General Counsel
TO: Nathan Foley, Senior Corporate Counsel
SUBJECT: Data Room Checklist — Project Lighthouse
CLASSIFICATION: Attorney-Client Privileged — Project Lighthouse
REFERENCE: NXS-LEGAL-2025-PL07

---

MEMORANDUM

TO: Nathan Foley, Senior Corporate Counsel
FROM: Rachel Dominguez, General Counsel
DATE: February 26, 2025
RE: Data Room Checklist — Project Lighthouse

Nathan,

Attached is the comprehensive data room checklist for Project Lighthouse. 14 categories, approximately 2,400 documents.

This memorandum outlines the complete document checklist for the Project Lighthouse virtual data room on the Intralinks platform. You are the project lead for document collection and upload. Please coordinate across departments to ensure all documents are loaded by the target date.

I. TARGET TIMELINE

- Data room fully loaded and ready for Meridian access: March 21, 2025
- This assumes we receive their LOI by March 17
- Wachtell Lipton to complete final review of all uploaded documents by March 21
- Data room will not be opened to Meridian until both (a) LOI execution and (b) Wachtell sign-off are complete

II. PRIORITY CATEGORIES (must be complete before data room opens)

Priority categories are those that any acquirer will review first and that directly impact valuation and deal structure:

Category 1: Financial
- 5 years audited financial statements (FY2020-FY2024)
- Interim Q1 2025 financials (as available at time of opening)
- Monthly management accounts for last 24 months
- Revenue by customer for last 36 months
- ARR bridge (new, expansion, contraction, churn) by quarter
- Tax returns (federal and state, 5 years)
- Budget and forecast models (FY2025-FY2027)
- Accounts receivable aging schedule
- Accounts payable aging schedule
- Capital expenditure schedule
- Debt schedule (currently no outstanding debt)
- Bank statements (last 12 months)
- Estimated documents: 380

Category 2: Legal
- All material contracts (contracts >$100K ACV or strategic significance)
- Standard form customer agreement (template)
- Standard form vendor agreement (template)
- Vendor contracts with change-of-control provisions (42 contracts)
- Customer contracts with change-of-control consent requirements (18 contracts)
- Litigation history (last 5 years — none pending)
- Regulatory filings
- Insurance policies
- Corporate formation documents
- Estimated documents: 420

Category 3: Intellectual Property
- Patent portfolio: 14 granted patents (full prosecution files)
- Patent portfolio: 6 pending applications (files and status)
- Trade secret inventory and protection protocols
- Software copyright registrations
- Open-source license compliance audit report
- Trademark registrations
- IP assignment agreements (all employees)
- Estimated documents: 180

Category 4: HR and Employment
- All executive employment agreements (14 executives)
- Change-of-control provisions in employment agreements
- Equity incentive plan (2019 plan, as amended)
- Option grant register (all outstanding grants)
- RSU vesting schedules
- Cap table (fully diluted, as of February 1, 2025)
- Benefits plan documents (medical, dental, vision, 401k)
- Retention bonus program documentation
- Organizational chart (detailed, by department)
- Compensation benchmarking data (last 2 surveys)
- Estimated documents: 340

III. SECONDARY CATEGORIES

Category 5: Technology — estimated 220 documents
- System architecture diagrams (application, infrastructure, data)
- Security policies and procedures
- SOC 2 Type I report (2024)
- SOC 2 Type II report (in progress, expected completion March 21)
- Penetration test reports (last 2 years)
- Disaster recovery plan and test results
- Technology stack inventory
- Third-party integration documentation
- SLA agreements with customers

Category 6: Customer — estimated 180 documents
- Customer list with ACV and contract dates
- Customer concentration analysis (top 20 accounts)
- Customer health scorecards
- NPS survey results (last 4 quarters)
- Customer retention and churn analysis
- Case studies and references

Category 7: Regulatory — estimated 90 documents
- Data privacy compliance documentation (GDPR, CCPA)
- Industry-specific regulatory compliance
- Government contracts (if any — currently none)
- Export control compliance

Category 8: Real Estate — estimated 60 documents
- Lease agreements (14th floor HQ, 13th floor expansion, warehouse)
- Lease assignment provisions and landlord consent requirements
- Space utilization reports
- Lease renewal options and termination provisions

Category 9: Insurance — estimated 40 documents
Category 10: Tax — estimated 120 documents
Category 11: Environmental — estimated 20 documents
Category 12: IP Licenses — estimated 80 documents
Category 13: Data Privacy — estimated 110 documents
Category 14: Corporate Governance — estimated 160 documents

IV. STAFFING AND COORDINATION

You are authorized to coordinate directly with department heads for document collection:
- Financial documents: Angela Torres (Controller) and Derek Hollis (FP&A Manager)
- HR documents: Vanessa Kline (VP People) and her team
- Technology documents: Marcus Webb (VP Engineering) and Lisa Chen (Director, Platform)
- Customer documents: Amanda Briggs (VP Customer Success)
- Sales documents: Kevin Orosco (VP Sales)

Important: when requesting documents from department heads, use the cover story that these are needed for "annual corporate governance review and insurance renewal." Do not reference Project Lighthouse or any potential transaction. Only you, me, and the executive team are read into the project.

Two full-time paralegals (Jennifer Okafor and Raj Sundaram) are assigned to the data room project through completion. They handle document formatting, redaction of personally identifiable information, and upload to Intralinks.

V. DOCUMENT FORMATTING STANDARDS

All documents must conform to the following standards before upload:
- Format: PDF (searchable, OCR applied to scanned documents)
- Naming convention: [Category Number]-[Subcategory]-[Document Name]-[Date]
- Redactions: PII for non-executive employees, personal contact information, SSNs
- Watermarks: "CONFIDENTIAL — Project Lighthouse" on all pages
- Version control: only final versions; drafts should not be uploaded

Wachtell Lipton will perform a final review of all uploaded documents for privileged content, inadvertent disclosure risk, and completeness. Their review requires 5 business days, which is factored into the March 21 target date. Wachtell has also requested that we flag any documents that reference Meridian Corp by name (there should be none in ordinary course documents, but the check is standard practice).

Intralinks data room access controls:
- Administrator access: Nathan Foley, Rachel Dominguez
- Upload access: Jennifer Okafor, Raj Sundaram (paralegals)
- Read-only access (Nextera side): David Aldric, Priya Vasquez, Maria Fuentes
- Meridian access: NOT YET PROVISIONED (will be configured upon LOI execution)
- Activity tracking: Intralinks logs all document views, downloads, and prints by user
- Access revocation: automatic upon deal termination or mutual agreement

VI. WEEKLY STATUS REPORTING

Please provide me with a weekly status report every Friday by 5 PM PST covering:
- Documents loaded (cumulative count and percentage)
- Category-by-category completion status
- Blockers or delays
- Staffing needs

First status report due: March 7, 2025.

VII. RISK ITEMS

The following risks have been identified and are being tracked:

1. SOC 2 Type II audit overlap: The audit runs March 3-21. Auditors may request documents that are also being collected for the data room. Coordinate with Angela Torres to avoid conflicting requests or alerting auditors to non-routine activity.

2. Engineering documentation lag: Technology category is only 40% complete. Marcus Webb's team is focused on v3 post-launch support and Axion integration. Assign a dedicated engineering writer to document collection for the next two weeks.

3. HR benefits plan complexity: Vanessa Kline's team manages 14 distinct benefit plans across multiple carriers. Document collection requires extracting plan documents, summary plan descriptions, and amendment histories. This is the most labor-intensive category.

Rachel Dominguez
General Counsel
Nextera Solutions, Inc.
